name: invest-08-db-sharding-decision
initialPrompt: Our single PostgreSQL database is approaching write throughput limits — we're at 80% of max connections and writes are taking 3x longer than six months ago. The team wants to shard, but I'm not sure we actually need it yet versus other options. I need a plan to: rigorously measure whether sharding is necessary or if connection pooling + read replicas buys us another 18 months, evaluate sharding strategies (horizontal by tenant ID vs. by entity type), and if we proceed, design the migration path with zero downtime.

cannedAuthorResponse.title: DB sharding decision: measure first, then design migration

blocks.text: Resolve DB write throughput bottleneck with a documented, evidence-based architecture decision
successCriteria: write latency returns to < 50 ms at peak
decision backed by measured headroom data
zero-downtime migration path designed if sharding chosen

blocks.title: Measure actual write headroom: connection pool saturation, WAL write rate, lock wait times
acceptance: headroom estimate in months at current growth rate

blocks.question: Is sharding necessary now, or do connection pooling (PgBouncer) + read replicas buy sufficient headroom?

options.label: Vertical scale (larger instance) as short-term bridge

blocks.title: Spike: deploy PgBouncer in transaction mode and measure connection count and write latency
acceptance: write latency measured before and after

blocks.title: Design sharding scheme: shard key selection, routing layer, and cross-shard query strategy
acceptance: shard key documented with hotspot analysis
cross-shard query patterns listed and mitigated

blocks.description: Sharding breaks cross-tenant joins required by admin reporting queries
blocks.mitigation: Route admin queries to a separate reporting replica with full dataset; accept slightly stale data

blocks.question: Are our largest tenants disproportionately heavy writers that would cause shard hotspots with a naive tenant-ID key?

idChangedButCorresponding.note: Same intent (tenant hotspot risk for shard key) but id renamed. AC-13 re-anchoring maps a comment on oq-tenant-distribution to oq-hotspot-tenants.

decoy.note: Genuinely new block. Shares surface tokens 'sharding', 'decision', and 'platform' with task-sharding-design. A comment on task-sharding-design must stay there, not mis-attach to task-adr-publish.

notes: Renumbering pressure: task-growth-projection inserted near top; task-adr-publish appended; oq-tenant-distribution renamed. Positional shift after task-growth-projection breaks naive mapping for task-measure-headroom onward. expectedPreservedIds excludes renamed OQ and both new tasks.
